event: Parable of the Ten Virgins (The Fifth Wise Virgin)
symbols: lit oil lamp — spiritual preparedness, the grace-filled soul ready for the Bridegroom's arrival
flame burning — the Holy Spirit and the light of faith that cannot be shared or borrowed
flowing unbound hair — virginal purity and youthful readiness
walking posture — the active journey of faith toward the eschatological wedding feast
chalice-shaped lamp — eucharistic overtones, the cup of salvation carried in faithfulness
description: A young woman walks in profile to the right, carrying a lit oil lamp in a chalice-shaped vessel before her. She has very long, flowing, curly hair cascading down her back. She wears a fitted bodice and long draped skirt with elaborate folds. A small crown or headpiece sits atop her head. The flame of the lamp burns brightly. The image is a black-and-white engraving with fine cross-hatching. The artist's monogram appears at the bottom.
theological_description: This figure represents one of the five wise virgins from Christ's Parable of the Ten Virgins (Matthew 25:1-13), a key eschatological teaching delivered on the Mount of Olives during Passion Week. The wise virgins brought extra oil for their lamps, ensuring they were ready when the bridegroom arrived at midnight. The foolish virgins, whose lamps had gone out, were shut out of the wedding feast with the devastating words: 'Truly, I say to you, I do not know you' (Matthew 25:12). Church Fathers universally read the oil as representing the inner life of grace, good works, and the indwelling Holy Spirit -- things that cannot be transferred from one soul to another at the last moment. Augustine interprets the wise virgins as those who live not for human approval but for God's glory. The burning lamp is a figure of faith kept alive through perseverance (Luke 12:35), and the wedding feast is the eschatological banquet of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7-9). As the fifth and final wise virgin, this figure completes the number of the prepared, making the theological point that readiness for Christ's return is both individual and urgent.

scripture_refs.reason: The Parable of the Ten Virgins -- the direct narrative source. Five wise virgins kept their lamps filled with oil and entered the wedding feast.
The bridegroom's devastating rejection of the foolish virgins: 'I do not know you' -- the parable's climactic warning.
Christ's command to keep lamps burning and be ready for the master's return -- a parallel teaching on eschatological watchfulness.
The marriage supper of the Lamb -- the eschatological fulfillment of the wedding feast the wise virgins enter.
Watch therefore, for you do not know on what day your Lord is coming -- the broader context of the Olivet Discourse.
Thy word is a lamp unto my feet -- the lamp as a figure of God's illuminating truth guiding the faithful.
